behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from the recommendation results regardless of their relevance score.
behaviors.justification: This tests filtering logic: the filter must exclude products where available=false. A Unit test with mock product objects containing availability flags directly tests the filter function's correctness. Mocking products isn't a liability here because we're testing the recommendation engine's responsibility (filtering), not data freshness. A Unit test catches the real failure (filter doesn't work or is missing). Integration would be overkill since we're not testing database staleness—that's a data quality concern outside the recommendation engine.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test product catalog with 20 products: 15 available and 5 marked available=false. Call the recommendation engine with a test user having no preference blockers. Assert that all 5 unavailable products are excluded from results regardless of their relevance scores. Repeat with unavailable products assigned high, medium, and low scores to ensure filtering is consistent across score distributions.

behaviors.description: The user preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked in their settings.
behaviors.justification: Like behavior 1, this is filtering logic: given a user preference object with blocked_categories, the filter must exclude matching products. A Unit test with mock user settings and product objects tests the core logic. Mocking settings doesn't miss the failure because we're testing the filter logic, not settings persistence or staleness. Integration would be overkill unless the concern is stale cached preferences—that's not part of the filtering behavior itself.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user profile with blocked_categories=['electronics', 'home', 'furniture']. Create a product catalog with 30 products spanning 8 categories including the blocked ones. Call the recommendation engine. Assert that no returned products have categories in ['electronics', 'home', 'furniture']. Test with multiple different blocked category sets and product distributions.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm ranks products that match the user's recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal.
behaviors.justification: This tests ranking algorithm logic. A Unit test directly verifies the algorithm's scoring: create two products with similar base relevance, one with browsing signal and one without, and verify the browsed product ranks higher. The test doesn't need I/O or external services because ranking is in-process algorithm logic. Mocking user browsing history and product scores doesn't hide the real failure: if the algorithm ignores browsing signals, the test will fail.
behaviors.test_description: Create two products (product_A and product_B) with identical relevance scores. Assign product_A a browsing signal (user viewed it recently, multiple times). Assign product_B no browsing signal. Call recommendation engine. Assert product_A appears higher in the ranked list than product_B. Repeat with multiple browsing patterns (single view, multiple views, recent vs. older) and varying base score distributions.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page of results, with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if the underlying data hasn't changed.
behaviors.justification: Deterministic ordering is an algorithm property, testable at Unit level. The failure would be: same request returns different results on different invocations. A Unit test runs the recommendation engine twice with identical input state and compares output—this catches non-determinism in sorting/ordering logic. No I/O needed. Mocking the product catalog doesn't hide this failure because we're testing algorithm determinism, not pagination algorithm correctness per se.
behaviors.test_description: Call the recommendation engine requesting page 2 with page_size=10. Capture the returned product list (IDs and order). Without changing any state, call immediately again with identical parameters. Assert both results are identical in content and order. Repeat across multiple users and page numbers (pages 1, 3, 5, 10). Test with users having varying history sizes.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before (serendipity requirement), avoiding a filter bubble.
behaviors.justification: Serendipity is an algorithm property: given a user with limited browsing history, the algorithm must diversify. A Unit test creates a user with narrow browsing (e.g., only 'shoes') and verifies the result includes categories outside that history. Mock products and user state are sufficient because we're testing the algorithm's diversity logic, not external data freshness. The test catches the real failure: algorithm only recommends within user's existing categories.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user who has browsed only in the 'shoes' category. Create a product catalog with products in 6 diverse categories: shoes, electronics, books, home, sports, beauty. Call the recommendation engine requesting top 20 results. Assert at least one product from a category other than 'shoes' is in the results. Repeat with different single-category browsing histories (e.g., user only browses 'electronics', user only browses 'books').

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than an empty result set.
behaviors.justification: Cold-start fallback is algorithm logic: when personalization signals are absent, use a fallback strategy (popularity). A Unit test creates a user with empty history and verifies recommendations are returned and sorted by popularity (not personalized). Mock user and product data are sufficient. The test catches the real failure: cold-start users get empty results or the fallback isn't triggered.
behaviors.test_description: Create a brand-new user with empty purchase_history and browsing_history. Call the recommendation engine. Assert that results are not empty. Assert returned products are sorted by overall popularity (compare ranking against a popularity_score field, not user-personalized signals). Create an established user for comparison; verify cold-start recommendations are different from personalized recommendations.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with.
behaviors.justification: Data persistence cannot be verified at Unit level—a Unit test mocking the write would pass even if writes fail in production. The real failure is: clicks aren't persisted, or are persisted incorrectly (wrong product ID, position, timestamp, or missing fields). Integration test with a real test database/event log is the minimum: write a click event, query storage, verify all fields are recorded correctly. This catches schema mismatches, persistence failures, and field mapping bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Record a click event with user_id=test_user, product_id=456, position=3, timestamp=T1. Write to test event storage (database or event log). Query the storage by user_id and product_id. Assert the stored record contains exact values: product_id=456, position=3, timestamp=T1 (within ±1 second accuracy), user_id=test_user. Test with multiple rapid successive clicks on different products to verify no data loss or corruption.

behaviors.description: The response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products.
behaviors.justification: Performance at scale cannot be measured at Unit level (tests with 100 products won't reveal 100k scaling issues). The real failure is: latency exceeds 200ms with realistic data volume. Integration with a large test database (100k products) is the minimum needed to measure realistic performance. Unit tests would miss this failure entirely. System testing with real production infrastructure could be valuable but isn't necessary—a large test database shows if the algorithm scales correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Populate a test database with 100,000 products, each with realistic attributes (category, relevance scores, availability status, popularity metrics). Create a test user with a realistic browsing history (50-500 items). Measure the end-to-end latency of calling the recommendation engine. Assert response time < 200ms. Repeat 10 times to account for JVM warmup and caching effects. Test with varying user history sizes (0, 50, 500, 5000 items) to verify scaling behavior remains sub-200ms.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased by the user, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires state update (purchase recorded) followed by algorithm behavior (deprioritization). A Unit test mocking purchase state could pass even if purchase state isn't persisted or retrieved correctly. The real failure is: algorithm receives stale or missing purchase state and continues recommending purchased products. Integration test with a test database is minimum: record purchase, call recommendation engine, verify deprioritization. This catches state persistence, retrieval, and algorithm responsiveness failures.
behaviors.test_description: Call the recommendation engine and capture the baseline ranking. Identify a mid-ranked product. Record a purchase event for that product and write to test database. Call the recommendation engine again. Assert the purchased product either is absent from top 5 results, or appears ranked lower than in the baseline. Repeat with multiple products (5-10 purchases) to verify deprioritization applies consistently across purchases.

behaviors.description: The recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list.
behaviors.justification: Determinism is an algorithm property: given identical input state, the algorithm must produce identical output. A Unit test captures a complete user state snapshot, calls the recommendation engine twice, and compares the ranked output. Any non-determinism in sorting, scoring, or result assembly is caught. Mocking external calls consistently (ML models, APIs) keeps the test at Unit level. The test catches real failures: seeded randomness not controlled, floating-point ordering instability, or non-deterministic collection iteration.
behaviors.test_description: Capture a complete user state snapshot: profile, preferences, browsing history (length 0 to 1000+), purchase history. Call the recommendation engine. Capture the result list (product IDs in order). Without changing any state, call immediately again with identical parameters. Assert both results are identical: same product IDs in same order. Test across diverse user states: new user (empty history), active user (large history), dormant user (old history, recent activity). Repeat each state 3 times to account for any transient non-determinism.
